---
title: "Linear Regression for Beijing Housing Prices"
author: "Rohan"
date: "2026-03-23"
output: html_document
---

# WD and libraries
```{r setup, include=FALSE}
setwd("C:/Users/rohan/OneDrive/Desktop/NUS Y1S2/BT2102/Assignment 3")
library(readxl)
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(car)

# Install ranger if not already installed
if (!require("ranger", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("ranger")
}
library(ranger)
```

# load data
```{r linearRegression, include=FALSE}
# Read file
df <- read_excel("cleaned_beijing_housing.xlsx")

# check data
head(df)
str(df)

# Prepare data for linear regression (exclude price_per_sqm)
model_data <- df %>%
  select(price, Lng, Lat, DOM, followers, totalPrice, square, livingRoom, 
         drawingRoom, kitchen, bathRoom, floor_num, buildingType, constructionTime,
         renovationCondition, buildingStructure, ladderRatio, elevator, 
         fiveYearsProperty, subway, district, communityAverage, 
         house_age) %>% 
  drop_na()
```

# Model 1: Simple Model
```{r model1}
model_simple <- lm(price ~ square + livingRoom + drawingRoom + kitchen + bathRoom + 
              elevator + subway + floor_num + house_age + renovationCondition + 
              buildingType + ladderRatio, 
            data = model_data)

summary(model_simple)
vif(model_simple)
```

# Model 2: Stepwise Model
```{r model2}
# full model
model_full <- lm(price ~ Lng + Lat + DOM + followers + totalPrice + square + 
                   livingRoom + drawingRoom + kitchen + bathRoom + floor_num + 
                   buildingType + constructionTime + renovationCondition + 
                   buildingStructure + ladderRatio + elevator + fiveYearsProperty + 
                   subway + district + communityAverage + house_age, 
                 data = model_data)

# backward stepwise regression
model_stepwise <- step(model_full, direction = "backward")

summary(model_stepwise)
```

# Random Forest Model & Value Score
```{r random_forest, fig.width=10, fig.height=7}
# Prepare data for Random Forest (same as linear regression)
rf_data <- model_data %>%
  select(price, Lng, Lat, DOM, followers, totalPrice, square, livingRoom, 
         drawingRoom, kitchen, bathRoom, floor_num, buildingType, constructionTime,
         renovationCondition, buildingStructure, ladderRatio, elevator, 
         fiveYearsProperty, subway, district, communityAverage, house_age)

# Build random Forest model
set.seed(42)
rf_model <- ranger(price ~ ., 
                   data = rf_data, 
                   num.trees = 500, 
                   importance = 'permutation',
                   write.forest = TRUE)

# Get importance scores
importance_scores <- importance(rf_model)

# Create data frame and normalize
importance_df <- data.frame(
  feature = names(importance_scores),
  importance = importance_scores
) %>%
  arrange(desc(importance)) %>%
  mutate(
    weight = importance / sum(importance),
    weight_pct = weight * 100
  )

# Display importance
cat("\n=== RANDOM FOREST FEATURE IMPORTANCE ===\n")
print(importance_df)

# Extract weights for VALUE SCORE features only
value_weights <- importance_df %>%
  filter(feature %in% c("square", "bathRoom", "kitchen", "elevator")) %>%
  arrange(desc(weight))

cat("\n=== VALUE SCORE WEIGHTS (DATA-DRIVEN) ===\n")
print(value_weights)

# Create weight lookup table
square_weight <- value_weights$weight[value_weights$feature == "square"]
bathroom_weight <- value_weights$weight[value_weights$feature == "bathRoom"]
kitchen_weight <- value_weights$weight[value_weights$feature == "kitchen"]
elevator_weight <- value_weights$weight[value_weights$feature == "elevator"]

# Calculate VALUE SCORE for each property
rf_data_with_value <- rf_data %>%
  mutate(
    value_score = (square * square_weight +
                   bathRoom * bathroom_weight +
                   kitchen * kitchen_weight +
                   elevator * elevator_weight) / communityAverage
  )

# Rank districts by average value score
summary_by_district <- rf_data_with_value %>%
  group_by(district) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_value_score = mean(value_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_price = mean(price, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_square = mean(square, na.rm = TRUE),
    count = n()
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_value_score))

cat("\n=== DISTRICTS RANKED BY VALUE SCORE ===\n")
print(summary_by_district)

# Plot importance
importance_df %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(feature, importance), y = importance)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#3B82F6") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "What Drives Beijing Property Prices?",
       subtitle = "Ranger Random Forest Feature Importance",
       x = "Feature",
       y = "Importance Score") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14))

importance_scores <- importance(rf_model)
print(importance_scores)

importance_df <- data.frame(
  feature = names(importance_scores),
  importance = importance_scores
) %>%
  mutate(weight = importance / sum(importance))

print(importance_df)

print(rf_model)
```

```{r test forest, fig.width=10, fig.height=7}
# Split data 80/20
set.seed(42)
train_indices <- sample(1:nrow(rf_data), 0.8 * nrow(rf_data))
train_data <- rf_data[train_indices, ]
test_data <- rf_data[-train_indices, ]

# Train on 80%
rf_model_train <- ranger(price ~ ., 
                         data = train_data, 
                         num.trees = 100, 
                         importance = 'permutation')

# Test on 20%
test_predictions <- predict(rf_model_train, test_data)$predictions

# Calculate test R²
test_ss_res <- sum((test_data$price - test_predictions)^2)
test_ss_tot <- sum((test_data$price - mean(test_data$price))^2)
test_r2 <- 1 - (test_ss_res / test_ss_tot)
train_r2 <- rf_model_train$r.squared

# Display results
cat("\n=== MODEL VALIDATION ===\n")
cat("Training R²:", round(train_r2, 4), "\n")
cat("Test R²:", round(test_r2, 4), "\n")
cat("Difference (overfitting check):", round(train_r2 - test_r2, 4), "\n")

if (test_r2 > 0.90) {
  cat("\n✅ EXCELLENT: Model generalizes well to unseen data\n")
} else if (test_r2 > 0.80) {
  cat("\n✅ GOOD: Model performs well with minor overfitting\n")
} else if (test_r2 > 0.70) {
  cat("\n⚠️ WARNING: Significant overfitting detected\n")
} else {
  cat("\n❌ PROBLEM: Model is severely overfit\n")
}
```

# Key Insights & Explanations
```{r insights}
cat("
=== TOP PRICE DRIVERS (Linear Regression) ===

1. totalPrice (+80.89 CNY per unit) - STRONGEST
   Transaction value dominates pricing

2. communityAverage (+0.27 CNY per unit) - VERY STRONG
   Market baseline/neighborhood matters

3. renovationCondition (+1,084 CNY) - VERY STRONG
   Better renovation = higher price

4. Lat (Latitude: +3,341 CNY)
   North-south location significantly affects price

5. bathRoom (+1,540 CNY per bathroom)
   More bathrooms = more expensive

6. kitchen (+1,470 CNY per kitchen)
   Kitchens add value

7. DOM (+16.93 CNY per day)
   Faster sales = higher perceived value

8. followers (+22.16 CNY per follower)
   Buyer interest indicates desirability

9. Lng (Longitude: -1,451 CNY)
   East-west location has negative effect

10. elevator (+758.6 CNY)
    Elevator access adds value

=== COUNTERINTUITIVE FINDINGS ===

1. square (-434.8 CNY per sqm) - NEGATIVE (confounded)
   BUT Random Forest shows square is 2nd most important!
   Explanation: Linear regression coefficient is negative when 
   controlling for totalPrice + communityAverage (confounding).
   Random Forest correctly identifies size as critical for prediction.
   
2. floor_num (-69.36 CNY per floor) - UNEXPECTED
   Higher floors slightly decrease price (cultural preference 
   for ground floor in China for family practicality)

3. constructionTime (-72.05 CNY per year) - REVERSED
   Older buildings add value (established neighborhoods)
   New construction clustered in speculative zones

4. buildingType (-384.4 CNY) & buildingStructure (-525 CNY)
   Different types command different premiums

5. fiveYearsProperty (-1,610 CNY) - OLDER OWNERS = LOWER PRICE
   Early speculators from bubble era selling at losses

=== MODEL QUALITY ===

Linear Regression R² = 0.853 (explains 85.3% of variation)
Random Forest Validation: Confirms top drivers identified

CONCLUSION: Beijing property prices are fundamentally driven by:
- Market tier (totalPrice, communityAverage)
- Practical amenities (bathrooms, kitchens, elevators)
- Property condition (renovation quality)
- Location (latitude/longitude)
")

```

```{r new csv}
# 1. Generate LR Predictions (using your stepwise model)
df$LR_Pred <- predict(model_stepwise, df)

# 2. Generate RF Predictions (using your ranger model)
# Note: ranger requires a specific way to call predictions
rf_predictions_obj <- predict(rf_model, df)
df$RF_Pred <- rf_predictions_obj$predictions

# 3. Export to a temporary CSV
write.csv(df %>% select(LR_Pred, RF_Pred), "predictions_export.csv", row.names = FALSE)
```
